lesson.menu_desc: Keeping in touch & making plans to reconnect -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 14 -- Staying in Touch
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 14 -- Staying in Touch

reading.rtitle: How travelers keep the bond alive
paras: Every good trip leaves you with a handful of people you genuinely do not want to lose. You share a long dinner, a delayed train or a sunrise hike, and suddenly a stranger feels like an old friend. The hard part is not meeting them; it is staying in touch once you are back home and life takes over. Many of us promise to keep in touch and then, slowly and silently, we drift apart.

The travelers who manage to reconnect tend to do one simple thing: they turn a vague promise into a concrete plan. Instead of a weak "let's stay in touch", they say something specific. "If I make it to Lisbon next spring, I'll let you know." "As soon as I land, I'll drop you a line." "Once things calm down at work, I'll reach out and we'll have a proper catch-up." Notice the pattern: a small condition or time clause, then a clear promise. The plan does the remembering for you.

It also helps to lower the stakes. You do not need a grand reunion to reconnect; a quick message to check in on someone, a photo, or a short voice note keeps the bond warm. Make a little effort every few weeks and you will rarely fall out of touch. The friends you keep from your travels are not the ones you met by chance; they are the ones you chose, on purpose, to look up again.

gist.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Meeting interesting people abroad is the hardest part of any trip.
People who stay in touch after travel turn vague promises into concrete plans and make small, regular efforts.
You should only reconnect with travel friends if you can plan a big reunion.

items: According to the text, the hardest part of a friendship made while traveling is meeting the person.
f
The hard part is staying in touch once you are back home, not meeting them.
The travelers who reconnect tend to replace a vague promise with a concrete plan.
t
Instead of a weak "let's stay in touch", they say something specific with a condition or time clause.
The writer says you need a grand reunion before you are allowed to reconnect.
f
You do not need a grand reunion; a quick message or voice note keeps the bond warm.
Making a small effort every few weeks helps you avoid falling out of touch.
t
Make a little effort every few weeks and you will rarely fall out of touch.

items: Think of someone you met while traveling. Have you stayed in touch, or did you drift apart? Why?
What is your honest way of keeping in touch once you are home -- messages, photos, calls, or nothing at all?
The text says a plan "does the remembering for you". Do you agree? What concrete plan could you make to reconnect with someone?
Now that you have more free time, who is one long-lost friend you would like to look up again, and how will you reach out?
I will arrive in Lisbon. Then I will let you know. (as soon as)
as soon as
Maybe the flight is on time. In that case I will call you from the airport. (if)
if
We will set a date. After that, I will book the tickets. (once)
once
I will not relax. First I have to hear back from you. (until)
until
The trip ends. Before that, I will get everyone's number. (before)
before

answerkey.title: Reveal the rephrase answer key
list: As soon as I arrive in Lisbon, I'll let you know.
If the flight is on time, I'll call you from the airport.
Once we set a date, I'll book the tickets.
I won't relax until I hear back from you.
Before the trip ends, I'll get everyone's number.
answerkey.note: Form check: after if / when / as soon as / once / until / before, use the PRESENT tense -- never "will". The will goes only in the main (result) clause: "As soon as I land (present), I'll text you (will)."

bank.label: Useful language for staying in touch
items: Let's not be strangers.
I'll keep you posted.
Drop me a line when you land.
Let's set a date before I forget.
I'll reach out as soon as things calm down.
We should have a proper catch-up soon.
Don't be a stranger -- check in any time.
As soon as I'm back, I'll send you the photos.

listenings.text: I want to tell you about the best lesson my travels ever taught me. For years, I was terrible at staying in touch. I would meet wonderful people on a trip, promise to keep in touch, and then quietly let the friendship drift apart. Life at home would take over, and a month later I had lost touch with someone I really liked. Then I changed one small habit. Now, before I even say goodbye, I make a concrete plan out loud. I say something like, as soon as I get home, I'll drop you a line, or if you ever come to Brazil, you'll stay with us. The promise has a hook, a real condition, so my brain remembers it. And every few weeks, I just check in on one of them. A photo, a short message, nothing grand. Since I started doing this, I almost never fall out of touch. The friends I made on the road are still in my life, because I chose to look them up on purpose.

This is a quick voice note for my friend Pricila. Hi, it's me. I'm so glad we met on that trip. Listen, I don't want us to be one of those pairs who say let's stay in touch and then vanish, so let me make this real. As soon as I finish this big project at work, I'll reach out and we'll set a proper date for a video call. If you decide to travel north next year, you have a place to stay with me, no question. And once you book anything, just keep me posted, because I would love to plan part of it together. Until then, I'll send you a photo every now and then so you don't forget my face. Take care of yourself, and let's promise each other one thing: we won't lose touch. Talk soon.

extra_audio.text: First, before they said goodbye, they promised to keep in touch. As soon as she lands, she will drop her sister a line. When things calm down, she will reach out for a proper catch-up. She will keep everyone posted the moment her plans change. Finally, they agreed to look each other up so they never drift apart again.
